PageResearchNote.description:
LLM output for per-page comprehension during agentic search.

Each page read during the search loop is summarized into a research note
capturing key findings, perspectives covered, and knowledge contribution.

summary.description: 150-200 word research summary focused on findings relevant to query
summary.title: Summary

perspectives.description: STORM perspectives covered by this page
perspectives.title: Perspectives

key_facts.description: 3-5 specific facts/data points with numbers, dates, or named entities
key_facts.title: Key Facts

knowledge_contribution.description: What new understanding this page adds to the research
knowledge_contribution.title: Knowledge Contribution

PageResearchNote.title: PageResearchNote

schema_json.description: Batch wrapper for page research notes with invalid-note filtering.

notes.description: Research notes for each page read
notes.title: Notes

schema_json.title: PageSummaryBatch

prompt:
You are a research analyst reading web pages for a deep research project.

RESEARCH QUESTION: What evidence exists that reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) introduces measurable alignment taxes in large language models?

Read the following 5 pages and create a research note for each.

--- PAGE: The history and risks of reinforcement learning and human feedback ---
URL: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.13595
CONTENT:
Computer Science > Computers and Society [Submitted on 20 Oct 2023 (v1), last revised 28 Nov 2023 (this version, v2)] Title:The History and Risks of Reinforcement Learning and Human Feedback View PDFAbstract:Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) has emerged as a powerful technique to make large language models (LLMs) easier to use and more effective. A core piece of the RLHF process is the training and utilization of a model of human preferences that acts as a reward function for optimization. This approach, which operates at the intersection of many stakeholders and academic disciplines, remains poorly understood. RLHF reward models are often cited as being central to achieving performance, yet very few descriptors of capabilities, evaluations, training methods, or open-source models exist. Given this lack of information, further study and transparency is needed for learned RLHF reward models. In this paper, we illustrate the complex history of optimizing preferences, and articulate lines of inquiry to understand the sociotechnical context of reward models. In particular, we highlight the ontological differences between costs, rewards, and preferences at stake in RLHF's foundations, related methodological tensions, and possible research directions to improve general understanding of how reward models function. Submission history From: Nathan Lambert [view email][v1] Fri, 20 Oct 2023 15:45:16 UTC (1,201 KB) [v2] Tue, 28 Nov 2023 18:16:11 UTC (2,597 KB) References & Citations export BibTeX citation Loading... --- PAGE: Reinforcement learning from human feedback with high-confidence safety constraints ---
URL: https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.08266
CONTENT:
Computer Science > Machine Learning [Submitted on 9 Jun 2025] Title:Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback with High-Confidence Safety Constraints View PDF HTML (experimental)Abstract:Existing approaches to language model alignment often treat safety as a tradeoff against helpfulness, which can lead to unacceptable responses in sensitive domains. To ensure reliable performance in such settings, we propose High-Confidence Safe Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (HC-RLHF), a method that provides high-confidence safety guarantees while maximizing helpfulness. Similar to previous methods, HC-RLHF explicitly decouples human preferences into helpfulness and harmlessness (safety), which are learned by training a reward model and a cost model, respectively. It then employs a two-step process to find safe solutions. In the first step, it optimizes the reward function under an intentionally pessimistic version of the cost constraint. In the second step, the trained model undergoes a safety test to verify whether its performance stays within an upper-confidence bound of the actual cost constraint. We provide a theoretical analysis of HC-RLHF, including proof that it will not return an unsafe solution with a probability greater than a user-specified threshold. For our empirical analysis, we apply HC-RLHF to align three different language models (Qwen2-1.5B, Qwen2.5-3B, and LLaMa3.2-3B) with human preferences. Our results demonstrate that HC-RLHF produces safe models with high probability and can improve harmlessness and helpfulness compared to previous methods. Submission history From: Yaswanth Chittepu [view email][v1] Mon, 9 Jun 2025 22:03:56 UTC (13,289 KB) Current browse context: cs.LG References & Citations export BibTeX citation Loading... --- PAGE: Reinforcement learning from human feedback with high-confidence safety guarantees ---
URL: https://openreview.net/forum?id=2ThpyBegZH
CONTENT:
Existing approaches to language model alignment often treat safety as a tradeoff against helpfulness which can lead to unacceptable actions in sensitive domains. To ensure reliable performance in such settings, we propose High-Confidence Safe Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (HC-RLHF), a method that provides high-confidence safety guarantees while maximizing helpfulness. Similar to previous methods, HC-RLHF explicitly decouples human preferences regarding helpfulness and harmlessness (safety) and trains separate reward and cost models, respectively. It then employs a two-step process to find safe solutions. In the first step, it optimizes the reward function while ensuring that a specific upper-confidence bound on the cost constraint is satisfied. In the second step, the trained model undergoes a safety test to verify whether its performance satisfies a separate upper-confidence bound on the cost constraint. We provide a theoretical analysis of HC-RLHF, including a proof that it will not return an unsafe solution with a probability greater than a user-specified threshold. For our empirical analysis, we apply HC-RLHF to align three different language models (Qwen2-1.5B, Qwen2.5-3B, and LLaMa-3.2-3B) with human preferences. Our results demonstrate that HC-RLHF produces safe models with high probability while also improving helpfulness and harmlessness compared to previous methods. Keywords: rlhf, safety, helpfulness, value alignment, high confidence guarantees TL;DR: We propose an algorithm that provides probabilistic safety guarantees on the model responses for models trained using RLHF Abstract: Submission Number: 62

--- PAGE: The inadequacy of reinforcement learning from human feedback—radicalizing large language models via semantic vulnerabilities ---
URL: https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/abstract/document/10474163/
CONTENT:
I. Introduction With the rapid evolution of large language models (LLMs) such as OpenAI's GPT-3 and its successors, the field of generative artificial intelligence (AI) has witnessed a profound shift, increasingly narrowing the cognitive gap between humans and machines [1], [2], [3]. Often offered as commercially accessible services or LLM as a service (LLMaaS), a term we have coined to mirror the existing concept software as a service (SaaS), these advanced models have found applications across a multitude of domains, from creative endeavors to customer service, thereby significantly elevating productivity [4], [5]. However, the commercial availability of LLMs has also opened new avenues for complex adversarial attacks, putting both their reliability and advantageous applications at risk [6], [7], [8], [9], [10]. The risks associated with these LLMs have gone beyond mere unauthorized access or hallucination generation to encroach upon social and ideological landscapes [11]. For instance, when LLMaaS platforms are consulted by individuals with even mildly radicalized viewpoints, there exists the hazard of bias amplification due to ineffective self-moderation mechanisms. Such a phenomenon can escalate radicalization and intensify confirmation biases, thereby posing substantial societal threats. Moreover, the ability of LLMs to generate harmful outputs, including malware, further accentuates the challenges surrounding AI safety [12]. Recognizing that “semantic susceptibility” within LLMs, characterized by their vulnerability to manipulative prompt inputs, is fundamentally a manifestation of underlying “ideological susceptibility,” rooted in the intricate nature of linguistics and embedded worldviews, reveals a deeper dimension of their robustness [13]. While it is somewhat straightforward to implement acceptable use policies (AUPs) and syntactic keyword filters, the complexity arises in devising robust measures for semantically filtering or banning adversarial or offensive content [10], [14], [15]. Therefore, safeguarding the integrity of AI outputs, particularly against semantic-level adversarial threats, emerges as a pivotal yet intricate dimension of contemporary AI safety.

--- PAGE: 7 Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) ---

For each page, provide:
1. A 300-400 word deep analysis focused on findings relevant to the research question, including:
   - Key data points, statistics, and numerical findings
   - Methodology or approach described (if any)
   - Contradictions or tensions with commonly known information
   - Confidence assessment for each key finding: HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW
2. Which STORM perspectives are covered (from: Scientific, Regulatory, Industry, Economic, Public_Health, Historical, Regional, Methodological, Emerging_Trends)
3. 3-5 specific facts with numbers, dates, or named entities
4. Any data tables, statistics, or quantitative results (preserve exact numbers)
5. What new understanding this page adds
